text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A Patel
City Health Medical Centre

12 Brookfield Road, London, SE5 8HL
Dear Dr A Patel
Jordan Evans, DOB: 14-03-1999, Hospital No: H937420 NHS No. 8426195073
Flat 3, 21 Rowan Court, London, SE15 3DY

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures with prior normal MRI and mixed semiology; previous EEGs inconclusive.

MRI 2019 reported no structural abnormality.

Previous medication: Levetiracetam (irritability, continued events), sodium valproate (weight gain), carbamazepine (dizziness), lamotrigine (partial benefit only), lacosamide (fatigue), clobazam PRN (sedation), topiramate (cognitive slowing), zonisamide (appetite loss).

Not taken: perampanel, brivaracetam, pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide.

Other history: University esports club lead; prolonged screen exposure and highly irregular sleep–wake pattern around tournaments. No known cardiac disease; past echocardiogram 2021 unremarkable.

I reviewed Jordan Evans in clinic today, accompanied by a teammate. They report episodes occurring on most days of the week, especially after late-night practice blocks and during periods of sleep curtailment. Typical events include brief behavioural arrest, loss of awareness for under a minute, and post-event confusion lasting 10–15 minutes; on rarer occasions there is limb jerking witnessed by peers. They note that symptoms are more likely after back-to-back matches and energy drink use. No clear injury reported, but they describe two near-falls in the last month. Headaches occur intermittently following tournament weekends.

Current medication: None regularly for seizures; uses melatonin intermittently (self-initiated) with variable effect; vitamin D supplement.

We discussed the potential impact of prolonged continuous gameplay, erratic sleep, dehydration, and stimulants (caffeine/energy drinks) as reported triggers. They are motivated to adjust team schedules to allow consistent sleep and breaks. They prefer to revisit investigations before re-trying medication, noting prior side effects.

Plan:
- Arrange repeat routine EEG with sleep deprivation protocol and consider ambulatory EEG if initial study non-diagnostic.
- Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D, B12/folate, and glucose profile.
- Sleep hygiene and screen-time structure: patient to trial fixed lights-out, screen breaks (20:20:20 approach), hydration targets, and limiting energy drinks, as discussed.
- Safety counselling provided regarding gaming arena exits, heights/cabling, cycling, swimming, and bathing; advised supervision where appropriate.
- We agreed no driving per current guidance given events on most days of the week; patient acknowledges and will inform DVLA/employer as applicable.
- Follow-up in 8–10 weeks or earlier if events escalate or injury occurs. We will consider medication reintroduction pending EEG/bloods and patient preference (options discussed in principle without commitment today).

Thank you for your continued care. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: episodes occurring on most days of the week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states the patient has seizure episodes occurring "on most days of the week." There is no explicit numeric count per day or week. This implies a frequent pattern but not strictly daily or a specific number. According to the rules, when the frequency is vague and not quantifiable, use "multiple". Therefore, the best normalized label is "multiple per week" as the highest discernible frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: episodes occurring on most days of the week

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (1 per day)
